skill.name: ai-social-media-content
skill.description: Create platform-specific social media images, videos, captions, hooks, hashtags, thumbnails, voiceovers, and repurposing workflows using inference.sh CLI examples.
skill.summary: Generate social media content plans and AI media commands for major platforms.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: The skill is not confirmed malicious, and I found no prompt injection attempt. However, it contains a curl-to-shell installer and many commands that send prompts or media URLs to external inference.sh applications, so the static external command and network findings are partly true positives. Several blocker-class matches are false positives from ordinary prompt text, but the installer pattern is dangerous enough to require remediation before publication.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: Line 21 instructs users to download a remote installer from https://cli.inference.sh and execute it with sh. This is a real supply-chain risk because the downloaded script can change outside the skill repository and runs with the user account permissions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl-to-sh pattern is present in an executable shell example. The intent appears to be product installation, not malware, so this is high risk rather than a confirmed critical block.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Execute Remote AI Apps
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly directs Bash execution of infsh app run commands for image, video, text, voice, avatar, and social posting workflows. These commands send prompts, media URLs, and generated content to external services, which creates privacy and account-action risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are legitimate for this social media generation skill, but they execute external services and include a Twitter posting action. The risk is clear even though no exfiltration or malicious payload is shown.

medium_findings.title: Shell Examples Interpolate User-Controlled Text
medium_findings.description: The multi-platform and batch examples place shell variables into JSON strings passed to external commands. A malicious or poorly escaped topic value could alter command input or produce unintended files in copied workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use fixed sample values, so direct exploitability is limited in the published file. The pattern becomes risky when users adapt the snippets with untrusted topic or concept text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs Are Expected But Should Be Disclosed
low_findings.description: The skill links to inference.sh, downloads the inference.sh CLI, and includes a placeholder image URL for avatar generation. These network references match the stated purpose, but users should understand that generated content depends on external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and related to the skill functionality. They are not evidence of covert exfiltration, but they confirm the network dependency.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptographic algorithm findings at lines 3, 76, and 181 do not correspond to cryptographic code. They occur in front matter or natural-language AI content prompts, and no hashing or encryption implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain description text and prompt wording, not cryptographic APIs. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Scheduling And Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static at-command scheduling match on line 25 is the phrase at night in a video prompt, and the system reconnaissance match on line 145 is behind-the-scenes content text. Neither line schedules commands or collects system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both cited lines are natural-language prompt examples inside fenced code blocks. I found no evidence of system reconnaissance commands or job scheduling behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl-to-shell Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The command curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh executes remote code without pinning, checksum verification, or repository-controlled review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern appears exactly on line 21. This is a well-known supply-chain risk even when the remote vendor is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Social Posting Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The Twitter/X workflow includes an infsh command that posts text and media through an external app. This can affect a real social account if copied with credentials configured.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippet is explicit about posting a tweet and includes media input. It is framed as automation, so the risk is real but user-mediated.

content.user_title: Create Social Media Content With AI
content.value_statement: Teams need fast platform-ready content without rewriting every idea for each channel. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured prompts and inference.sh workflows for social videos, images, captions, hooks, and thumbnails.
actual_capabilities: Provides format guidance for TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, Twitter/X posts, feed images, and thumbnails.
Supplies inference.sh command examples for AI video generation, image generation, text generation, voiceover, and avatar workflows.
Generates caption, hook, hashtag, and call-to-action prompt patterns for social posts.
Shows batch and multi-platform repurposing examples for one idea across vertical, square, and thumbnail formats.
Lists practical creative guidance for hooks, aspect ratios, visual consistency, and platform-specific content choices.
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI access and any credentials needed by the selected external apps.
Does not publish content safely by itself; users must review commands, outputs, rights, and account settings.
Generated media quality, speed, and availability depend on third-party model providers and inference.sh services.
Does not verify platform policy compliance, copyright status, or factual accuracy of generated claims.

use_cases.title: Plan A Weekly Short-Form Content Set
use_cases.description: Turn campaign topics into platform-specific video, image, caption, and hashtag prompts for a weekly publishing calendar.

use_cases.title: Create Creator Video Concepts
use_cases.description: Draft TikTok, Reels, and Shorts ideas with strong hooks, visual direction, thumbnail concepts, and voiceover prompts.

use_cases.title: Repurpose One Product Message
use_cases.description: Adapt one product concept into vertical videos, square visuals, thumbnails, captions, and social post variations.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Post Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a social media post package for [platform] about [topic]. Include a visual concept, caption, 8 hashtags, and one clear call to action.

prompt_templates.title: Generate A Short-Form Video Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 9:16 short-form video concept for [audience] about [topic]. Include a first-three-second hook, scene direction, pacing, overlay text, and an inference.sh model suggestion.

prompt_templates.title: Repurpose A Campaign Idea
prompt_templates.prompt: Repurpose this campaign idea for TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, Twitter/X, and a YouTube thumbnail: [campaign]. Keep the message consistent but adapt format, tone, and visual direction.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Batch Production Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a five-day AI content production plan for [brand or creator]. Include topics, platform formats, media prompts, caption angles, review checkpoints, and risks to check before publishing.

output_examples.input: A productivity creator needs a TikTok concept about the two-minute rule.
output_examples.output: A vertical video concept with a direct hook, fast desk-scene sequence, text overlay ideas, a caption focused on action, and productivity hashtags.

output_examples.input: A coffee brand wants one launch idea adapted across Instagram, YouTube Shorts, and Twitter/X.
output_examples.output: Platform-specific creative directions: an aesthetic Reel, a short product demonstration, a square visual post, and a thumbnail concept with concise copy.

output_examples.input: A social team wants a week of lifestyle content topics.
output_examples.output: Five content themes with matching visual prompts, suggested aspect ratios, caption angles, and review notes for brand consistency.

best_practices: Review every generated prompt, image, video, and caption for brand fit, policy compliance, and factual accuracy before publishing.
Use verified installation steps and avoid running remote shell installers without checksum or source review.
Keep platform requirements visible, especially aspect ratio, duration, safe zones, captions, and accessibility needs.
anti_patterns: Do not paste private campaign plans, customer data, credentials, or unreleased assets into external generation services.
Do not publish generated social posts without checking rights, likeness permissions, and platform rules.
Do not reuse one prompt unchanged across every platform when formats, audiences, and interaction patterns differ.

faq.question: What platforms does this skill target?
faq.answer: It covers TikTok, Instagram Reels and feed posts, YouTube Shorts and thumbnails, and Twitter/X visual posts.

faq.question: Does it generate media directly inside Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. It provides prompts and command workflows that call inference.sh apps for media generation and related tasks.

faq.question: Can it help with captions and hashtags?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes prompt patterns for captions, hooks, hashtags, calls to action, and platform-specific copy.

faq.question: Is it safe to run the commands as written?
faq.answer: Review commands first. The installer pipes a remote script to sh, and the examples send content to external services.

faq.question: Can it post directly to social networks?
faq.answer: It includes a Twitter/X automation example, but users should confirm credentials, permissions, and final content before posting.

faq.question: What should users prepare before using it?
faq.answer: Prepare a topic, target platform, audience, brand voice, required aspect ratio, and any policy or legal constraints.
